Active and inactive μ-opioid receptors[[5]](#cite_note-Zhorov_2000-5)

The **μ-opioid receptors** (using the Greek letter [mu](/wiki/Mu_(letter) "Mu (letter)"), abbreviated **MOR**) are a class of [opioid receptors](/wiki/Opioid_receptor "Opioid receptor") with a high affinity for [enkephalins](/wiki/Enkephalin "Enkephalin") and [beta-endorphin](/wiki/Beta-endorphin "Beta-endorphin"), but a low affinity for [dynorphins](/wiki/Dynorphin "Dynorphin"). They are also referred to as μ(*mu*)-opioid [peptide](/wiki/Peptide "Peptide") (MOP) receptors. The prototypical μ-opioid receptor [agonist](/wiki/Agonist "Agonist") is [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), the primary psychoactive alkaloid in [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium") and for which the receptor was named, with [mu](/wiki/Mu_(letter) "Mu (letter)") being the first letter of [Morpheus](/wiki/Morpheus "Morpheus"), the compound's namesake in the original Greek. It is an inhibitory [G-protein coupled receptor](/wiki/G-protein_coupled_receptor "G-protein coupled receptor") that activates the [Gi alpha subunit](/wiki/Gi_alpha_subunit "Gi alpha subunit"), inhibiting [adenylate cyclase](/wiki/Adenylate_cyclase "Adenylate cyclase") activity, lowering [cAMP](/wiki/Cyclic_adenosine_monophosphate "Cyclic adenosine monophosphate") levels.

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mu-opioid_receptor&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structure")]

The structure of the inactive μ-opioid receptor has been determined with the antagonists [β-FNA](/wiki/Beta-Funaltrexamine "Beta-Funaltrexamine")[[6]](#cite_note-6) and [alvimopan](/wiki/Alvimopan "Alvimopan").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Many structures of the active state are also available, with agonists including [DAMGO](/wiki/DAMGO "DAMGO"),[[8]](#cite_note-Koehl_2018-8) [β-endorphin](/wiki/%CE%92-Endorphin "Β-Endorphin"),[[9]](#cite_note-9) [fentanyl](/wiki/Fentanyl "Fentanyl") and [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine").[[10]](#cite_note-10) The structure with the agonist [BU72](/wiki/BU72 "BU72") has the highest resolution,[[11]](#cite_note-11) but contains unexplained features that may be experimental [artifacts](/wiki/Artifact_(error) "Artifact (error)").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) This large body of evidence has enabled [structure-based design](/wiki/Drug_design "Drug design") of a new class of opioids with [functional selectivity](/wiki/Functional_selectivity "Functional selectivity").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Splice variants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mu-opioid_receptor&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Splice variants")]

Three variants of the μ-opioid receptor are well characterized, though [reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction](/wiki/Reverse_transcription_polymerase_chain_reaction "Reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction") has identified up to 10 total splice variants in humans.[[15]](#cite_note-DortchCarnes_2007-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Pan_2005-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Xu_2017-17)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **μ1** | More is known about the μ1 opioid receptor than the other variants. |
| **μ2** | [TRIMU 5](/wiki/TRIMU_5 "TRIMU 5") is a selective agonist of the μ2 receptor.[[18]](#cite_note-Eisenberg_1994-18) |
| **μ3** | The μ3 variant was first described in 2003.[[19]](#cite_note-Cadet_2003-19) It is responsive to opiate [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") but not [opioid peptides](/wiki/Opioid_peptide "Opioid peptide").[[20]](#cite_note-Stefano_2004-20) |

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mu-opioid_receptor&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Location")]

They can exist either presynaptically or postsynaptically depending upon cell types.

The μ-opioid receptors exist mostly [presynaptically](/wiki/Presynaptic "Presynaptic") in the [periaqueductal gray](/wiki/Periaqueductal_gray "Periaqueductal gray") region, and in the superficial [dorsal horn](/wiki/Posterior_horn_of_spinal_cord "Posterior horn of spinal cord") of the [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord") (specifically the [substantia gelatinosa of Rolando](/wiki/Substantia_gelatinosa_of_Rolando "Substantia gelatinosa of Rolando")). Other areas where they have been located include the external plexiform layer of the [olfactory bulb](/wiki/Olfactory_bulb "Olfactory bulb"), the [nucleus accumbens](/wiki/Nucleus_accumbens "Nucleus accumbens"), in several layers of the [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex"), and in some of the [nuclei](/wiki/Nucleus_(neuroanatomy) "Nucleus (neuroanatomy)") of the [amygdala](/wiki/Amygdala "Amygdala"), as well as the nucleus of the solitary tract.

Some MORs are also found in the intestinal tract. Activation of these receptors inhibits peristaltic action which causes constipation, a major side effect of μ agonists.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Activation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mu-opioid_receptor&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Activation")]

MOR can mediate acute changes in neuronal excitability via suppression of presynaptic release of [GABA](/wiki/Gamma-aminobutyric_acid "Gamma-aminobutyric acid"). Activation of the MOR leads to different effects on dendritic spines depending upon the agonist, and may be an example of [functional selectivity](/wiki/Functional_selectivity "Functional selectivity") at the μ-receptor.[[22]](#cite_note-Liao_2005-22) The physiological and pathological roles of these two distinct mechanisms remain to be clarified. Perhaps, both might be involved in opioid addiction and opioid-induced deficits in cognition.

Activation of the μ-opioid receptor by an agonist such as [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") causes [analgesia](/wiki/Analgesia "Analgesia"), [sedation](/wiki/Sedation "Sedation"), slightly reduced [blood pressure](/wiki/Blood_pressure "Blood pressure"), [itching](/wiki/Itching "Itching"), [nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea"), [euphoria](/wiki/Euphoria "Euphoria"), [decreased respiration](/wiki/Decreased_respiration "Decreased respiration"), [miosis](/wiki/Miosis "Miosis") (constricted pupils), and decreased bowel motility often leading to [constipation](/wiki/Constipation "Constipation"). Some of these effects, such as analgesia, sedation, euphoria, itching and decreased respiration, tend to lessen with continued use as tolerance develops. Miosis and reduced bowel motility tend to persist; little tolerance develops to these effects.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The canonical MOR1 isoform is responsible for morphine-induced analgesia, whereas the alternatively spliced MOR1D isoform (through heterodimerization with the [gastrin-releasing peptide receptor](/wiki/Gastrin-releasing_peptide_receptor "Gastrin-releasing peptide receptor")) is required for morphine-induced itching.[[23]](#cite_note-Liu_2011-23)

## Deactivation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mu-opioid_receptor&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Deactivation")]

As with other [G protein-coupled receptors](/wiki/G_protein-coupled_receptor "G protein-coupled receptor"), signalling by the μ-opioid receptor is terminated through several different mechanisms, which are upregulated with chronic use, leading to rapid [tachyphylaxis](/wiki/Tachyphylaxis "Tachyphylaxis").[[24]](#cite_note-Martini_2007-24) The most important regulatory proteins for the MOR are the [β-arrestins](/wiki/Arrestin "Arrestin") [arrestin beta 1](/wiki/Arrestin_beta_1 "Arrestin beta 1") and [arrestin beta 2](/wiki/Arrestin_beta_2 "Arrestin beta 2"),[[25]](#cite_note-Zuo_2005-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Marie_2006-26)[[27]](#cite_note-DuPen_2007-27) and the [RGS proteins](/wiki/Regulator_of_G_protein_signalling "Regulator of G protein signalling") [RGS4](/wiki/RGS4 "RGS4"), [RGS9-2](/wiki/RGS9 "RGS9"), [RGS14](/wiki/RGS14 "RGS14"), and [RGSZ2](/wiki/RGS17 "RGS17").[[28]](#cite_note-Garzon_2005-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Hooks_2008-29)

Long-term or high-dose use of opioids may also lead to additional mechanisms of tolerance becoming involved. This includes downregulation of MOR gene expression, so the number of receptors presented on the cell surface is actually reduced, as opposed to the more short-term desensitisation induced by β-arrestins or RGS proteins.[[30]](#cite_note-Sirohi_2008-30)[[31]](#cite_note-LopezGimenez_2008-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Kraus_2009-32) Another long-term adaptation to opioid use can be upregulation of glutamate and other pathways in the brain which can exert an opioid-opposing effect, so reduce the effects of opioid drugs by altering downstream pathways, regardless of MOR activation.[[33]](#cite_note-GarciaFuster_2008-33)[[34]](#cite_note-Ueda_2009-34)

## Tolerance and overdoses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mu-opioid_receptor&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Tolerance and overdoses")]

Fatal [opioid overdose](/wiki/Opioid_overdose "Opioid overdose") typically occurs due to [bradypnea](/wiki/Bradypnea "Bradypnea"), [hypoxemia](/wiki/Hypoxemia "Hypoxemia"), and decreased [cardiac output](/wiki/Cardiac_output "Cardiac output") ([hypotension](/wiki/Hypotension "Hypotension") occurs due to [vasodilation](/wiki/Vasodilation "Vasodilation"), and [bradycardia](/wiki/Bradycardia "Bradycardia") further contributes to decreased cardiac output).[[35]](#cite_note-Blok_2017-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Hughes_2012-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) A [potentiation](/wiki/Potentiator "Potentiator") effect occurs when opioids are combined with [ethanol](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)"), [benzodiazepines](/wiki/Benzodiazepine "Benzodiazepine"), [barbiturates](/wiki/Barbiturate "Barbiturate"), or other [central depressants](/wiki/Depressant "Depressant") which can result in rapid loss of consciousness and an increased risk of fatal overdose.[[35]](#cite_note-Blok_2017-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Hughes_2012-36)

Substantial tolerance to respiratory depression develops quickly, and tolerant individuals can withstand larger doses.[[38]](#cite_note-Algera_2021-38) However, tolerance to respiratory depression is quickly lost during withdrawal and may be completely reversed within a week. Many overdoses occur in people who return to their previous dose after having lost their tolerance following cessation of opioids. This puts addicts who receive medical treatment for opioid addiction at great risk of overdose when they are released, as they may be particularly vulnerable to relapse.

Less commonly, massive overdoses have been known to cause [circulatory collapse](/wiki/Circulatory_collapse "Circulatory collapse") from vasodilation and bradycardia.[[39]](#cite_note-Krantz_2021-39)

Opioid overdoses can be rapidly reversed through the use of opioid [antagonists](/wiki/Receptor_antagonist "Receptor antagonist"), [naloxone](/wiki/Naloxone "Naloxone") being the most widely used example.[[35]](#cite_note-Blok_2017-35) Opioid antagonists work by binding competitively to μ-opioid receptors and displacing opioid agonists. Additional doses of naloxone may be necessary and supportive care should be given to prevent hypoxic brain injury by monitoring vital signs.

[Tramadol](/wiki/Tramadol "Tramadol") and [tapentadol](/wiki/Tapentadol "Tapentadol") carry additional risks associated with their dual effects as [SNRIs](/wiki/Serotonin_norepinephrine_reuptake_inhibitor "Serotonin norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor") and can cause [serotonin syndrome](/wiki/Serotonin_syndrome "Serotonin syndrome") and seizures. Despite these risks, there is evidence to suggest that these drugs have a lower risk of respiratory depression compared to morphine.[[40]](#cite_note-Houmes_1992-40)
